name: Coriander
description: A fast-growing aromatic herb widely used in Indian and Asian cooking. Both the fresh leaves (cilantro) and dried seeds are used as culinary spices. Easy to grow in pots and a great starter herb for beginners.
soil_type: Well-draining, fertile loamy mix
care_instructions: Sow seeds directly into the pot and thin seedlings to 10-15 cm apart. Water regularly but avoid waterlogging. Harvest outer leaves frequently to prevent early bolting. Place in a sunny windowsill or balcony. Avoid transplanting as it causes bolting.
common_problems: Bolts quickly in hot weather — sow in cooler months or provide shade. Susceptible to aphids and powdery mildew. Yellowing leaves indicate overwatering.
fertilizer_needs: Light balanced fertilizer once a month

name: Curry Leaf Tree
description: An essential herb in South Indian cuisine, the curry leaf tree produces aromatic leaves used extensively in tempering and cooking. It thrives in warm climates and can be grown in large pots on a sunny balcony.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining loam with good organic content
care_instructions: Plant in full sun to partial shade. Water moderately, letting the top inch of soil dry out between waterings. Prune regularly to encourage bushy growth and more leaf production. Feed monthly during growing season. Protect from frost.
common_problems: Scale insects and sooty mold are common. Leaf drop occurs in cold weather. Yellow leaves may indicate iron deficiency — use an iron chelate supplement.
fertilizer_needs: Monthly with a balanced or nitrogen-rich fertilizer during growing season

name: Indian Borage
description: Also known as Karpooravalli or Cuban Oregano, this thick-leaved succulent herb is prized in Indian folk medicine for treating coughs and colds. It has a strong camphor-like oregano fragrance and is very easy to propagate from cuttings.
soil_type: Well-draining potting mix with added perlite
care_instructions: Water only when the topsoil feels dry. Prefers warm, bright conditions. Pinch stem tips regularly to encourage bushy, compact growth. Propagate easily from stem cuttings in soil or water. Avoid overwatering — leaves store moisture.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering is the main risk. Spider mites may appear in dry indoor conditions. Leggy growth indicates insufficient light.
fertilizer_needs: Monthly balanced liquid fertilizer during growing season

name: Ashwagandha
description: One of Ayurveda's most revered adaptogenic herbs, Ashwagandha is known for its stress-reducing and immunity-boosting properties. It is a hardy, drought-tolerant shrub that produces small orange-red berries. Grown primarily for its medicinal roots.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining soil with low organic matter
care_instructions: Requires full sun — at least 6-8 hours daily. Water sparingly; allow the soil to dry out completely between waterings. It is drought-tolerant once established. Harvest roots after 150-180 days. Avoid heavy clay soils that retain moisture.
common_problems: Root rot in waterlogged or humid conditions. Leaf spot fungal disease can appear in wet weather. Whiteflies and aphids may infest plants indoors.
fertilizer_needs: Minimal — once a month with a low-nitrogen fertilizer during growing season

name: Giloy
description: Known as Guduchi in Ayurveda, Giloy is a climbing vine celebrated for its potent immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties. The heart-shaped leaves, fleshy stems, and roots are all used medicinally. It is a vigorous grower in warm climates.
soil_type: Loamy, well-draining soil rich in organic matter
care_instructions: Provide a trellis or support structure for this climbing vine. Water regularly during the growing season and reduce in winter. Grows best in partial shade to full sun. Prune vigorous stems to manage spread. Propagate easily from stem cuttings.
common_problems: Aphids and scale insects on young stems. Powdery mildew in humid, stagnant air. Root rot if drainage is poor.
fertilizer_needs: Monthly organic compost or balanced fertilizer during growing season
mature_size: Vine up to 10 m; 1-2 m in containers

name: Phalaenopsis Orchid
description: The most popular orchid for home growing, Phalaenopsis (Moth Orchid) produces stunning long-lasting blooms in shades of white, pink, purple, and striped patterns. It thrives in bright indirect light and rewards minimal care with months of flowering.
soil_type: Orchid bark mix or sphagnum moss — never regular potting soil
care_instructions: Water by submerging the pot in water for 10-15 minutes weekly, then let drain fully. Never let roots sit in standing water. Provide bright indirect light — an east-facing window is ideal. After blooms drop, cut the spike just above a node to encourage reblooming. Mist aerial roots occasionally.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering or poor drainage. Crown rot if water collects in the center. Spider mites and scale insects in dry conditions. Bud blast (dropping buds) caused by drafts or sudden temperature changes.
fertilizer_needs: Orchid fertilizer (30-10-10) diluted to quarter strength, bi-weekly during active growth
repotting_frequency: Every 1-2 years, after flowering, when roots outgrow the pot

name: String of Pearls
description: A stunning trailing succulent with bead-like round leaves that resemble a string of pearls. Perfect for hanging baskets where its trailing vines can cascade dramatically. Each bead stores water, making it drought-tolerant and low-maintenance.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining cactus/succulent mix
care_instructions: Allow soil to dry out completely between waterings — overwatering is the number one killer. Water thoroughly then wait 2-3 weeks before watering again. Provide bright indirect light. Avoid cold drafts. Hanging planters allow the beautiful trailing vines to display fully.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering causes mushy, wrinkled pearls. Shriveled pearls indicate underwatering or too much direct sun. Mealybugs can hide between pearls — treat with neem oil.
fertilizer_needs: Diluted liquid succulent fertilizer once in spring and once in summer

name: Aglaonema
description: Also known as Chinese Evergreen, this lush tropical plant is one of the best low-light houseplants available. The pink and green variegated variety adds a bold pop of color to any interior and is nearly indestructible with basic care.
soil_type: Well-draining peat-based or pearlite-enriched potting mix
care_instructions: Water when the top inch of soil feels dry. Tolerates low light but brighter indirect light enhances leaf color vibrancy. Avoid cold drafts and direct sun, which scorches leaves. Wipe leaves with a damp cloth occasionally to keep them dust-free and healthy.
common_problems: Yellow leaves from overwatering or root rot. Brown leaf tips from low humidity or fluoride in tap water — use filtered water. Spider mites and mealybugs in dry indoor conditions.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during spring and summer

name: Cactus
description: A delightful mixed cactus arrangement featuring columnar and globe-shaped cacti that occasionally produce beautiful white blooms. Cacti are the ultimate low-maintenance plants — perfect for those who tend to forget to water.
soil_type: Cactus/succulent mix with added coarse sand or perlite for drainage
care_instructions: Place in the sunniest spot available — a south-facing windowsill is ideal. Water thoroughly once every 3-4 weeks in summer, once a month or less in winter. Ensure the pot has drainage holes. Handle with thick gloves when repotting. Minimal care needed.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering in soggy soil is the primary risk. Mealybugs and scale insects hide in spines — treat with rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab. Etiolation (stretching toward light) indicates insufficient sun.
fertilizer_needs: Cactus fertilizer once in spring and once in early summer

name: Coleus
description: A dazzling foliage plant loved for its vividly colored leaves in combinations of purple, magenta, green, and red. Coleus brings bold tropical color to shady spots where flowering plants struggle. It grows quickly and is easy to propagate from cuttings.
soil_type: Rich, moist, well-draining potting mix with compost
care_instructions: Keep soil evenly moist — never let it dry out completely. Pinch off flower spikes as they appear to redirect energy into foliage and maintain vibrant colors. Bright indirect light produces the best color intensity. Propagate easily in water or soil.
common_problems: Wilting from underwatering or heat stress. Aphids and mealybugs on tender stems. Fungal diseases in poor air circulation. Leggy growth in low light.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced fertilizer every 2 weeks during growing season

name: Caladium
description: Caladiums are prized for their stunning heart-shaped leaves that come in breathtaking combinations of red, pink, white, and green. They are tropical bulb plants that go dormant in winter, making them seasonal showstoppers for shaded indoor and outdoor spaces.
soil_type: Rich, moist, well-draining potting mix with high organic content
care_instructions: Keep soil consistently moist during active growth. Requires high humidity — use a pebble tray with water or a humidifier nearby. Protect from direct sun to avoid leaf scorch. In late autumn, reduce watering to allow dormancy; store tubers dry over winter and replant in spring.
common_problems: Tuber rot from overwatering in cool conditions. Leaf scorch from direct sunlight. Spider mites in low humidity. Fungal leaf spots in poor air circulation.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced liquid fertilizer every 2 weeks during active growth

name: Ficus Bonsai
description: A classic indoor bonsai variety, the Ficus Microcarpa (Chinese Banyan) is one of the most forgiving bonsai species for beginners. Trained and shaped over years, it develops a thick, dramatic trunk with aerial roots and dense canopy — a true living sculpture.
soil_type: Bonsai mix — akadama, pumice, and lava rock in equal parts
care_instructions: Water thoroughly when the top layer of soil starts to dry — never let bonsai soil dry out completely. Mist leaves in low humidity. Prune regularly to maintain the desired shape. Wire branches to guide growth. Provide bright indirect light indoors or dappled outdoor light. Repot to refresh soil and trim roots.
common_problems: Leaf drop when moved or in drafts — place in a stable location. Root rot in overly wet soil. Spider mites, scale insects in dry conditions. Yellowing leaves indicate overwatering or poor light.
fertilizer_needs: Bonsai-specific fertilizer every 2 weeks during growing season, monthly in winter
repotting_frequency: Every 2-3 years to trim roots and refresh soil

name: Kalanchoe
description: A cheerful, compact flowering succulent that produces clusters of long-lasting blooms in red, orange, yellow, pink, or white. Kalanchoe is one of the easiest flowering houseplants to care for and can be coaxed into reblooming year after year.
soil_type: Cactus or succulent potting mix with excellent drainage
care_instructions: Water when the top inch of soil is dry — every 10-14 days. Provide bright indirect light for best flowering. After blooms fade, cut back flower stems and reduce watering and light for 6 weeks to trigger the next bloom cycle. Avoid cold windowsills in winter.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering is the primary risk. Powdery mildew in poor air circulation. Aphids and mealybugs on flower stems. Leggy growth in low light.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during the flowering period

name: Lemongrass
description: A tall, aromatic grass with a fresh citrus fragrance, widely used in Thai, Vietnamese, and Indian cooking. Lemongrass is also valued in herbal teas and aromatherapy. It grows vigorously in warm sunny spots and can be harvested continuously.
soil_type: Rich, loamy, well-draining soil with added compost
care_instructions: Plant in full sun — needs at least 6 hours of direct sunlight daily. Water regularly in summer, reducing in cooler months. Harvest the outer stalks by cutting at ground level. Divide large clumps every 2 years. In cold climates, bring pots indoors for winter.
common_problems: Rust fungus causes orange-red spots on leaves — improve air circulation and avoid wetting leaves. Mealybugs and spider mites in dry conditions. Browning tips are normal and can be trimmed.
fertilizer_needs: Nitrogen-rich fertilizer monthly during the growing season
repotting_frequency: Every 1-2 years or when clumps become too large

name: Croton
description: One of the most colorful foliage plants available, Croton dazzles with leaves splashed in combinations of red, orange, yellow, and green. It thrives in bright light, which intensifies its colors. A statement plant for sunny rooms and covered balconies.
soil_type: Rich, moist, well-draining potting mix
care_instructions: Provide bright indirect to direct sunlight — more light means more vibrant colors. Water when the top inch of soil is dry. Avoid cold drafts or sudden temperature changes, which cause leaf drop. Wipe leaves with a damp cloth monthly to remove dust. Mist occasionally to boost humidity.
common_problems: Leaf drop when moved or exposed to drafts or cold. Spider mites, mealybugs, and scale insects in dry conditions — treat with neem oil. Faded colors indicate insufficient light.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during spring and summer

name: English Ivy
description: A classic trailing and climbing vine with attractive variegated leaves. English Ivy is excellent for hanging baskets, topiary, or as a cascading accent. It is one of the best air-purifying plants and thrives in cooler indoor environments.
soil_type: Well-draining potting mix, slightly alkaline pH preferred
care_instructions: Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Prefers cool to moderate temperatures — avoid hot, dry rooms. Prune regularly to control growth and encourage density. Train on a frame or let it trail freely. Good for bathrooms or cool entryways.
common_problems: Spider mites thrive in warm, dry conditions — maintain humidity. Aphids on new growth. Bacterial leaf spot in overwatered conditions. Yellowing leaves from overwatering or root rot.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during spring and summer

name: Lemon Balm
description: A fragrant member of the mint family, Lemon Balm has a gentle lemon scent and is beloved for herbal teas, calming remedies, and culinary use. It grows quickly, is easy to care for, and makes a wonderful aromatic pot herb for sunny windowsills.
soil_type: Well-draining, moderately fertile potting mix
care_instructions: Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged. Water when the top of soil feels dry. Harvest leaves regularly to encourage bushy, productive growth. Trim flower buds before they open to keep energy focused on leaves. Divide and repot when the plant gets crowded.
common_problems: Powdery mildew in poor air circulation — improve ventilation. Aphids and whiteflies on tender new growth. Root rot if overwatered. Can spread aggressively outdoors if not contained.
fertilizer_needs: Light balanced liquid fertilizer every 4-6 weeks

name: Neem Tree
description: The Neem tree is one of the most revered medicinal trees in Ayurveda and traditional Indian medicine. Every part — leaves, bark, seeds, and oil — has potent antibacterial, antifungal, and insecticidal properties. It can be grown in large pots in warm, sunny locations.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining soil — tolerates poor soil conditions well
care_instructions: Requires full sun — at least 6 hours of direct sunlight daily. Water regularly when young; once established, it is highly drought-tolerant. Prune to control size and shape when grown in containers. Protect from frost. Leaves can be harvested for medicinal and pest-repellent uses.
common_problems: Mealybugs and scale insects on stems and leaves. Leaf miners create tunnels in foliage. Root rot in poorly draining or waterlogged soil. Yellowing and leaf drop in cold or waterlogged conditions.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced fertilizer monthly during the growing season

name: Haworthia
description: Often called the Zebra Plant due to its striking white horizontal stripes on dark green leaves, Haworthia is one of the most beginner-friendly succulents. It stays compact, tolerates low light better than most succulents, and thrives with minimal care.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining cactus/succulent mix
care_instructions: Water every 2-3 weeks in summer, less frequently in winter. Bright indirect light is ideal — it tolerates lower light better than most succulents. Always ensure good drainage; never let it sit in water. One of the most forgiving houseplants for beginners.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering or sitting in water. Mealybugs in the crevices between leaves — treat with neem oil. Leaf discoloration (reddish tint) from too much direct sun. Slow growth is normal.
fertilizer_needs: Diluted succulent fertilizer once in spring and once in summer

name: Jade Plant
description: A beloved long-lived succulent with thick, jade-green oval leaves and a woody trunk that develops character over years. In Feng Shui, the Jade Plant is considered a symbol of good luck and prosperity. It is easy to care for and can live for decades with proper care.
soil_type: Sandy, well-draining cactus/succulent mix
care_instructions: Water when the top 2 inches of soil feel dry — every 10-14 days in summer, less in winter. Provide full to bright indirect sunlight. Reduce watering in winter when growth slows. Propagate easily from leaf or stem cuttings. Avoid cold windowsills.
common_problems: Root rot from overwatering is the most common issue. Powdery mildew in poor air circulation. Mealybugs in leaf joints. Wrinkled leaves indicate underwatering. Dropping leaves from cold drafts or sudden temperature change.
fertilizer_needs: Balanced fertilizer every 3-4 months during spring and summer

name: Gardenia
description: Gardenia is celebrated for its intoxicatingly fragrant white blooms and glossy dark green leaves. It is one of the most rewarding — yet demanding — flowering plants to grow indoors. When happy, it produces waves of gorgeous, waxy flowers with an unforgettable scent.
soil_type: Acidic, well-draining potting mix (pH 5.0-6.5) — use ericaceous compost
care_instructions: Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged. Use a pebble tray or humidifier to maintain high humidity — essential for bud development. Provide bright indirect light. Avoid moving the plant once buds have formed, as this causes bud drop. Use acidic fertilizer and rainwater or filtered water if possible.
common_problems: Bud drop from low humidity, drafts, or temperature fluctuations. Yellowing leaves from alkaline water or soil — use ericaceous feed. Powdery mildew, aphids, whiteflies, and scale insects. Sooty mold following pest infestations.
fertilizer_needs: Acidic (ericaceous) fertilizer monthly during spring and summer
